summary: Gruber examines the practice of using bounding asterisks for emphasis (like *cough*) in published writing, noting its appearance in David Pogue's New York Times review of the Surface Pro. He argues this is an Internet-ism that originated as a plain-text substitute for italics in email, newsgroups, and forums. Using Google's Ngram Viewer, he traces the history: *sigh* first appeared in print in the mid-1960s (likely from Peanuts comics), while *cough* didn't appear until 1987, with both exploding after 1997 alongside Internet adoption. He connects this convention directly to his own design choice in Markdown's syntax for emphasis.
key_points: Bounding asterisks for emphasis originated as a plain-text substitute for italics in Internet communication contexts like email and forums
Google Ngram data shows *sigh* appearing in the 1960s (likely from Peanuts) and *cough* not until 1987, with both surging after 1997
The 1997 inflection point strongly correlates with mainstream Internet adoption, supporting the Internet-ism hypothesis
Gruber explicitly connects this convention to his design of Markdown's asterisks-for-emphasis syntax
The migration of Internet punctuation conventions into formal published writing (like the NYT) represents a shift in typographic norms
money_quotes: Using asterisks this way strikes me as an Internet-ism.

Using bounding asterisks is a substitute in plain text contexts, something we collectively started doing in email, newsgroups, web comments and forums, Twitter, and various other input fields where computer software doesn't allow proper italics.
Both though, exploded in usage starting in 1997, and have risen steadily ever since.
So I think I'm correct that this use of asterisks has been largely driven by Internet punctuation idioms.
What I wish I could do with Google's Ngram Search is look for instances of these words actually italicized, to see if that treatment has dropped as bounding asterisks have risen.
Hence my adoption of asterisks-for-emphasis in Markdown; it was already a widely-used convention for Internet users.
key_insight: The convention of using bounding asterisks for emphasis is an Internet-ism born from plain-text constraints that has migrated into formal published writing, and its history can be traced through Google Ngram data showing a clear post-1997 surge.
